---
title: "Plotting and visualization"
subtitle: "Inspect designs and timing without changing the modeling contract."
jupyter: python3
execute:
  freeze: auto
---

Visualization helpers turn the objects you already authored into diagnostic
figures: the realized design, the sampling grid, and the HRF shape that will be
convolved into the model.

## Plot a Realized Design

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

import fmrimod as fm
from fmrimod.hrf import get_hrf
from fmrimod.plotting import plot_hrf
from fmrimod.spec import compile as compile_spec
from fmrimod.spec import hrf
from fmrimod.visualization import plot_design_matrix, plot_sampling_frame

events = pd.DataFrame({
    "onset": [0.0, 10.0, 20.0, 30.0],
    "condition": ["face", "scene", "face", "scene"],
    "duration": 2.0,
})
sampling = fm.SamplingFrame(blocklens=60, TR=1.0)
event_model, _ = compile_spec(hrf("condition", basis="spmg1"), data=events, sampling_frame=sampling)
```

```{python}
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 3.0
fig, ax = plot_design_matrix(event_model, figsize=(7, 3))
plt.show()
```

## Plot the Sampling Frame

The sampling-frame plot is useful when a model has multiple runs or unusual
TRs. It reads the timing object already used by design construction.

```{python}
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 2.0
fig, ax = plot_sampling_frame(sampling, figsize=(7, 2))
plt.show()
```

## Plot an HRF

The `fmrimod.plotting` module covers HRF and regressor plots. Use it to inspect
an HRF object before it is convolved into a design.

```{python}
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 3.0
ax = plot_hrf(get_hrf("spmg1"), normalize=True)
plt.show()
```

## Next

- [Design matrices](design-matrices.qmd)
- [Overview and workflow](overview-workflow.qmd)
